title: Testing PostgreSQL High Availability on a Single VM: The Complete Guide
description: Learn how to set up and test a full PostgreSQL High Availability cluster — including Patroni, etcd, and HAProxy — on just one machine using native services and multiple PostgreSQL instances. Covers automatic failover, graceful switchover, node recovery, read/write splitting, and three different HA approaches with step-by-step instructions, real commands, and expected outputs.
author: E-Learning Platform
heroAlt: Single VM running multiple PostgreSQL instances with Patroni, etcd, and HAProxy as native services — simulating a multi-node HA cluster on one machine

sections.title: Introduction
content: PostgreSQL High Availability traditionally requires multiple physical or virtual machines: at least 3 nodes for a proper quorum-based setup. This creates a high barrier to entry. You need to provision VMs, configure networking between them, manage firewalls, and deal with infrastructure before you can even start learning how HA works.

There is a better way. A single Linux VM can run multiple PostgreSQL instances with different ports and data directories, each managed by Patroni and coordinated by etcd — all as native processes. You get the full HA experience — leader election, automatic failover, read/write splitting — without any container overhead.

In this comprehensive guide, you will learn three approaches to testing PostgreSQL HA on a single VM, from a full production-grade Patroni stack down to lightweight manual replication. Every command includes its expected output so you know exactly what should happen.

infoBox.content: 💡 All examples use PostgreSQL 17/18, Patroni 4.x, etcd 3.5, and HAProxy 2.9 installed directly on Ubuntu 22.04+ or Debian 12+. Everything runs as native systemd services or background processes. No Docker required.

items.label: What You Will Learn
text: ✅ How to simulate a 3-node PostgreSQL cluster on a single machine using native OS services
✅ Three different HA approaches: Patroni+etcd+HAProxy, pg_auto_failover, and manual streaming replication
✅ How to test automatic failover by killing the leader and watching a replica get promoted
✅ How to test graceful switchover for planned maintenance
✅ How to test node recovery when a failed node rejoins the cluster
✅ How to test read/write splitting through HAProxy
✅ What HA features you can and cannot test on a single VM
✅ Best practices for resource limits, port maps, and data persistence

sections.title: Prerequisites
content: Before starting, make sure your environment meets these requirements.

rows: Each PG instance needs CPU for WAL and replication
PostgreSQL uses shared_buffers; 3 instances need ~3 GB minimum
Each PG instance stores WAL and data separately

items.label: Software Requirements
text: • PostgreSQL 17/18 — install via apt from the official PostgreSQL APT repository
• etcd 3.5 — install via apt or download the binary from GitHub
• HAProxy 2.9 — install via apt
• Python 3.10+ and pip — for installing Patroni
• psql client (comes with PostgreSQL)
• curl and wget — for health checks
items.code:
# Install prerequisites on Ubuntu/Debian:

# Add PostgreSQL APT repository:
sudo sh -c 'echo "deb http://apt.postgresql.org/pub/repos/apt $(lsb_release -cs)-pgdg main" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/pgdg.list'
curl -fsSL https://www.postgresql.org/media/keys/ACCC4CF8.asc | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/postgresql.gpg

# Install PostgreSQL, HAProxy, etcd, and Python:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y postgresql-17 postgresql-17 postgresql-client-17 \
    haproxy etcd python3 python3-pip python3-venv

# Install Patroni:
pip3 install patroni[etcd3]

# Verify installations:
psql --version
# Expected: psql (PostgreSQL) 17.x

etcd --version
# Expected: etcd Version: 3.5.x

haproxy -v
# Expected: HAProxy version 2.9.x

patroni --version
# Expected: patroni 4.x

items.label: Port Availability Check
text: The HA stack uses these ports on your host. Make sure none are already in use:
items.code:
# Check if ports are free on Linux:
ss -tulpn | grep -E '(5000|5001|5432|5433|5434|2379|2380|8008|8404)'

# All should return no matches. If ports are in use, stop the conflicting services
# or use different ports in the configuration files.

# Also check if any systemd services are already running on these ports:
systemctl list-units --type=service --state=running | grep -E '(postgres|etcd|haproxy)'

sections.title: Architecture Overview: How Multiple Instances Simulate Multiple Nodes
content: A PostgreSQL HA cluster needs three things: multiple PostgreSQL instances with streaming replication, a distributed consensus store to coordinate leader election, and a load balancer for read/write routing. On separate VMs, each service runs on its own machine. On a single VM, each PostgreSQL instance runs as a separate process with its own port, data directory, and configuration.

The key insight is that PostgreSQL supports running multiple instances on the same host. Each instance uses a different port (5432, 5433, 5434), a different data directory (/var/lib/postgresql/17/main1, main2, main3), and separate systemd services. From Patroni's perspective, the instances are indistinguishable from separate VMs — Patroni communicates with each via its REST API on different ports (8008, 8009, 8010).

items.label: Service Architecture Diagram
text: Here is how the services map to system processes for the full Patroni-based approach:

rows: Load balancer — single entry point for apps

items.label: How It Works
text: 1. The three etcd processes form a 3-node distributed consensus cluster using the local host IP (127.0.0.1) with different ports. etcd stores the cluster state: who is the leader, what is the current timeline, and configuration.
2. The three Patroni processes each manage one PostgreSQL instance. Patroni monitors PostgreSQL health via its REST API and communicates with etcd to participate in leader election.
3. When the cluster starts, the three Patroni nodes elect a leader. The leader's PostgreSQL is writable. The other two become replicas and stream WAL from the leader.
4. HAProxy checks each Patroni node's REST API every 3 seconds. The write port (5000) goes to the leader only. The read port (5001) load-balances across replicas.

5. If the leader process crashes or is stopped, etcd detects the lock is lost. The remaining Patroni nodes hold a new election. One replica is promoted to leader. HAProxy detects the change and routes writes to the new leader.

sections.title: Method 1: Patroni + etcd + HAProxy (Full HA Stack)
content: This is the most complete and production-realistic testing approach. Patroni handles automated failover, etcd provides distributed consensus, and HAProxy gives you a stable connection endpoint. This is the same stack used by companies like GitLab, Zalando, and Postgres.ai.

We will set up 7 OS-level services: 3 etcd nodes, 3 Patroni-managed PostgreSQL instances, and 1 HAProxy load balancer. Everything runs as native systemd services or direct processes on a single Linux VM.

subsections.title: Step 1: Create Data Directories and Users
subsections.content: Each PostgreSQL instance needs its own data directory, and each etcd node needs its own data directory.

items.label: Create directories for all services
items.code:
sudo mkdir -p /var/lib/etcd/{etcd1,etcd2,etcd3}
sudo mkdir -p /var/lib/postgresql/{patroni1,patroni2,patroni3}
sudo mkdir -p /etc/patroni
sudo mkdir -p /etc/haproxy

# Verify directories:
ls -la /var/lib/etcd/
ls -la /var/lib/postgresql/

subsections.title: Step 2: Configure the etcd Cluster (3 Nodes)
content: We will run 3 etcd instances on different ports, all on localhost. Each instance gets its own configuration file and data directory.

items.label: Create etcd1 configuration
items.code:
sudo tee /etc/etcd1.conf.yml << 'EOF'
name: etcd1
data-dir: /var/lib/etcd/etcd1
listen-peer-urls: http://127.0.0.1:12380
listen-client-urls: http://127.0.0.1:12379
advertise-client-urls: http://127.0.0.1:12379
initial-advertise-peer-urls: http://127.0.0.1:12380
initial-cluster: etcd1=http://127.0.0.1:12380,etcd2=http://127.0.0.1:22380,etcd3=http://127.0.0.1:32380
initial-cluster-token: pg-ha-cluster
initial-cluster-state: new
EOF

echo "Created /etc/etcd1.conf.yml"

items.label: Create etcd2 configuration
items.code:
sudo tee /etc/etcd2.conf.yml << 'EOF'
name: etcd2
data-dir: /var/lib/etcd/etcd2
listen-peer-urls: http://127.0.0.1:22380
listen-client-urls: http://127.0.0.1:22379
advertise-client-urls: http://127.0.0.1:22379
initial-advertise-peer-urls: http://127.0.0.1:22380
initial-cluster: etcd1=http://127.0.0.1:12380,etcd2=http://127.0.0.1:22380,etcd3=http://127.0.0.1:32380
initial-cluster-token: pg-ha-cluster
initial-cluster-state: new
EOF

echo "Created /etc/etcd2.conf.yml"

items.label: Create etcd3 configuration
items.code:
sudo tee /etc/etcd3.conf.yml << 'EOF'
name: etcd3
data-dir: /var/lib/etcd/etcd3
listen-peer-urls: http://127.0.0.1:32380
listen-client-urls: http://127.0.0.1:32379
advertise-client-urls: http://127.0.0.1:32379
initial-advertise-peer-urls: http://127.0.0.1:32380
initial-cluster: etcd1=http://127.0.0.1:12380,etcd2=http://127.0.0.1:22380,etcd3=http://127.0.0.1:32380
initial-cluster-token: pg-ha-cluster
initial-cluster-state: new
EOF

echo "Created /etc/etcd3.conf.yml"

subsections.title: Step 3: Create Patroni Configuration Files
content: Each Patroni instance needs its own YAML configuration. The key differences are the PostgreSQL port, data directory, and REST API port.

items.label: Create patroni1.yml configuration
items.code:
sudo tee /etc/patroni/patroni1.yml << 'EOF'
scope: pg-ha-cluster
name: patroni1

restapi:
  listen: 127.0.0.1:18081
  connect_address: 127.0.0.1:18081

etcd3:
  hosts:
    - 127.0.0.1:12379
    - 127.0.0.1:22379
    - 127.0.0.1:32379

bootstrap:
  dcs:
    ttl: 30
    loop_wait: 10
    retry_timeout: 10
    maximum_lag_on_failover: 1048576
    postgresql:
      use_pg_rewind: true
      use_slots: true
      parameters:
        max_connections: 50
        shared_buffers: 256MB
        wal_level: replica
        hot_standby: on
        max_wal_senders: 10
        max_replication_slots: 10
        wal_log_hints: on

  initdb:
    - encoding: UTF8
    - data-checksums

  pg_hba:
    - host replication replicator 127.0.0.1/32 md5
    - host all all 127.0.0.1/32 md5
    - host all all 0.0.0.0/0 md5

postgresql:
  listen: 127.0.0.1:15432
  connect_address: 127.0.0.1:15432
  data_dir: /var/lib/postgresql/patroni1
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin
  pgpass: /tmp/pgpass
  authentication:
    replication:
      username: replicator
      password: replpass
    superuser:
      username: postgres
      password: super-pass-789
    rewind:
      username: replicator
      password: replpass
  parameters:
    unix_socket_directories: /var/run/postgresql

tags:
  nofailover: false
  noloadbalance: false
  clonefrom: false
  nosync: false
EOF

echo "Created /etc/patroni/patroni1.yml"

items.label: Create patroni2.yml configuration
items.code:
sudo tee /etc/patroni/patroni2.yml << 'EOF'
scope: pg-ha-cluster
name: patroni2

restapi:
  listen: 127.0.0.1:18082
  connect_address: 127.0.0.1:18082

etcd3:
  hosts:
    - 127.0.0.1:12379
    - 127.0.0.1:22379
    - 127.0.0.1:32379

postgresql:
  listen: 127.0.0.1:15433
  connect_address: 127.0.0.1:15433
  data_dir: /var/lib/postgresql/patroni2
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin
  pgpass: /tmp/pgpass
  authentication:
    replication:
      username: replicator
      password: replpass
    superuser:
      username: postgres
      password: super-pass-789
    rewind:
      username: replicator
      password: replpass
  parameters:
    unix_socket_directories: /var/run/postgresql
EOF

echo "Created /etc/patroni/patroni2.yml"

items.label: Create patroni3.yml configuration
items.code:
sudo tee /etc/patroni/patroni3.yml << 'EOF'
scope: pg-ha-cluster
name: patroni3

restapi:
  listen: 127.0.0.1:18083
  connect_address: 127.0.0.1:18083

etcd3:
  hosts:
    - 127.0.0.1:12379
    - 127.0.0.1:22379
    - 127.0.0.1:32379

postgresql:
  listen: 127.0.0.1:15434
  connect_address: 127.0.0.1:15434
  data_dir: /var/lib/postgresql/patroni3
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin
  pgpass: /tmp/pgpass
  authentication:
    replication:
      username: replicator
      password: replpass
    superuser:
      username: postgres
      password: super-pass-789
    rewind:
      username: replicator
      password: replpass
  parameters:
    unix_socket_directories: /var/run/postgresql
EOF

echo "Created /etc/patroni/patroni3.yml"

subsections.title: Step 4: Create the HAProxy Configuration
content: HAProxy checks each Patroni node's REST API to determine which is the leader (port 8008 returns 200 if the node is primary). The write port (5000) points only to the leader, while the read port (5001) distributes across all replicas.

items.label: Create /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg
items.code:
sudo tee /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg << 'HAPROXY_EOF'
global
    maxconn 1000
    log /dev/log local2
    stats socket /var/run/haproxy.sock mode 600 level admin
    stats timeout 30s

defaults
    mode tcp
    log global
    option tcplog
    timeout connect 5000ms
    timeout client 50000ms
    timeout server 50000ms
    timeout check 2000ms

# HAProxy Statistics Dashboard
listen stats
    mode http
    bind *:8404
    stats enable
    stats uri /
    stats refresh 5s
    stats auth admin:admin

# PostgreSQL Write (Primary) - Port 5000
listen pg-write
    bind *:5000
    mode tcp
    option httpchk GET /primary
    http-check expect status 200
    default-server inter 3s fall 3 rise 2 on-marked-down shutdown-sessions

    server patroni1 127.0.0.1:15432 check port 18081
    server patroni2 127.0.0.1:15433 check port 18082
    server patroni3 127.0.0.1:15434 check port 18083

# PostgreSQL Read (Replicas) - Port 5001
listen pg-read
    bind *:5001
    mode tcp
    balance roundrobin
    option httpchk GET /replica
    http-check expect status 200
    default-server inter 3s fall 3 rise 2

    server patroni1 127.0.0.1:15432 check port 18081
    server patroni2 127.0.0.1:15433 check port 18082
    server patroni3 127.0.0.1:15434 check port 18083
HAPROXY_EOF

echo "Created /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg"

subsections.title: Step 5: Create systemd Service Files
content: We will create systemd service files for each etcd and Patroni instance so they can be managed with `systemctl`.

items.label: Create etcd service files
items.code:
# etcd1 service
sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/etcd1.service << 'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=etcd1 - distributed consensus
Documentation=https://etcd.io/docs
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=notify
ExecStart=/usr/bin/etcd --config-file /etc/etcd1.conf.yml
Restart=always
RestartSec=5
LimitNOFILE=40000

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# etcd2 service
sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/etcd2.service << 'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=etcd2 - distributed consensus
Documentation=https://etcd.io/docs
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=notify
ExecStart=/usr/bin/etcd --config-file /etc/etcd2.conf.yml
Restart=always
RestartSec=5
LimitNOFILE=40000

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# etcd3 service
sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/etcd3.service << 'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=etcd3 - distributed consensus
Documentation=https://etcd.io/docs
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=notify
ExecStart=/usr/bin/etcd --config-file /etc/etcd3.conf.yml
Restart=always
RestartSec=5
LimitNOFILE=40000

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

echo "Created etcd1, etcd2, etcd3 systemd services"

items.label: Create Patroni service files
items.code:
# patroni1 service
sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/patroni1.service << 'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=Patroni1 - PostgreSQL HA manager
After=etcd1.service etcd2.service etcd3.service network.target
Requires=etcd1.service etcd2.service etcd3.service

[Service]
Type=simple
User=postgres
Group=postgres
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/patroni /etc/patroni/patroni1.yml
KillMode=process
Restart=always
RestartSec=30
LimitNOFILE=65536

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# patroni2 service
sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/patroni2.service << 'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=Patroni2 - PostgreSQL HA manager
After=etcd1.service etcd2.service etcd3.service network.target
Requires=etcd1.service etcd2.service etcd3.service

[Service]
Type=simple
User=postgres
Group=postgres
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/patroni /etc/patroni/patroni2.yml
KillMode=process
Restart=always
RestartSec=30
LimitNOFILE=65536

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# patroni3 service
sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/patroni3.service << 'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=Patroni3 - PostgreSQL HA manager
After=etcd1.service etcd2.service etcd3.service network.target
Requires=etcd1.service etcd2.service etcd3.service

[Service]
Type=simple
User=postgres
Group=postgres
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/patroni /etc/patroni/patroni3.yml
KillMode=process
Restart=always
RestartSec=30
LimitNOFILE=65536

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

echo "Created patroni1, patroni2, patroni3 systemd services"

subsections.title: Step 6: Set Permissions and Start the Stack
content: Set the correct ownership on all data directories, reload systemd, and start every service in the right order.

items.label: Set permissions
items.code:
# Set ownership for etcd data:
sudo chown -R etcd:etcd /var/lib/etcd

# Set ownership for PostgreSQL data:
sudo chown -R postgres:postgres /var/lib/postgresql

# Reload systemd to pick up new services:
sudo systemctl daemon-reload

items.label: Start Patroni instances (they will initialize PostgreSQL)
items.code:
# Start all 3 Patroni instances:
sudo systemctl start patroni1
sudo systemctl start patroni2
sudo systemctl start patroni3

# The first start takes 1-2 minutes as Patroni runs initdb and creates
# the PostgreSQL data directories. Watch the logs:
sudo journalctl -u patroni1 -f --no-tail &

# Wait for all nodes to initialize:
echo "Waiting 60 seconds for Patroni cluster to form..."
sleep 60

# Check the cluster state:
sudo -u postgres patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni1.yml list

# Expected:
# + Cluster: pg-ha-cluster ----------+---------+-----------+----+-----------+
# | Member   | Host             | Role    | State     | TL | Lag in MB |
# +----------+------------------+---------+-----------+----+-----------+
# | patroni1 | 127.0.0.1:15432  | Leader  | running   |  1 |           |
# | patroni2 | 127.0.0.1:15433  | Replica | streaming |  1 |         0 |
# | patroni3 | 127.0.0.1:15434  | Replica | streaming |  1 |         0 |
# +----------+------------------+---------+-----------+----+-----------+

items.label: Start HAProxy
items.code:
# Start HAProxy:
sudo systemctl start haproxy

# Enable all services to start on boot (optional):
sudo systemctl enable etcd1 etcd2 etcd3 patroni1 patroni2 patroni3 haproxy

# Verify all services are running:
sudo systemctl status etcd1 etcd2 etcd3 patroni1 patroni2 patroni3 haproxy --no-pager

# Check HAProxy stats:
curl -s -u admin:admin http://localhost:8404/ | grep -E '(UP|DOWN|MAINT|status)' | head -20

# Expected: the pg-write backend shows one server UP (the leader)
# and two servers DOWN. The pg-read backend shows all three UP.

subsections.title: Step 7: Verify Cluster Health
subsections.content: Run these checks to make sure everything is working correctly.

items.label: Check Patroni REST API on each node
items.code:
# Check each node's role and state:
echo "=== patroni1 (expected: primary) ==="
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:18081/health | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(f'State: {d[\"state\"]}, Role: {d[\"role\"]}')"

echo "=== patroni2 (expected: replica) ==="
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:18082/health | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(f'State: {d[\"state\"]}, Role: {d[\"role\"]}')"

echo "=== patroni3 (expected: replica) ==="
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:18083/health | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(f'State: {d[\"state\"]}, Role: {d[\"role\"]}')"

# Expected output (approximate):
# === patroni1 ===
# State: running, Role: primary
# === patroni2 ===
# State: running, Role: replica
# === patroni3 ===
# State: running, Role: replica

subsections.title: Step 8: Create Test Data
subsections.content: Create a test database with some data so we can verify it survives failover.

items.label: Connect to PostgreSQL through HAProxy and create test data
items.code:
# Connect to the write port (5000) — always goes to the leader:
psql -h localhost -p 5000 -U postgres -d postgres

# Inside psql, enter these commands:
# Password: super-pass-789

CREATE DATABASE ha_test;
\c ha_test

CREATE TABLE users (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(100),
    email VARCHAR(100),
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES
    ('Alice', 'alice@example.com'),
    ('Bob', 'bob@example.com'),
    ('Charlie', 'charlie@example.com');

SELECT * FROM users;

# Expected:
#  id |  name   |       email        |         created_at
# ----+---------+--------------------+----------------------------
#   1 | Alice   | alice@example.com  | 2026-05-21 10:00:00.123456
#   2 | Bob     | bob@example.com    | bob@example.com
#   3 | Charlie | charlie@example.com | charlie@example.com
# (3 rows)

\q

subsections.title: Step 9: Test Automatic Failover (Kill the Leader)
content: This is the most important test. We simulate a server crash by stopping the leader process. Patroni should detect the failure, hold a leader election, and promote a replica — all automatically.

items.label: Step 9a: Identify the current leader
items.code:
# Find which node is the leader:
sudo -u postgres patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni1.yml list

# Example output (yours may differ):
# + Member   | Role    | State     |
# +----------+---------+-----------+
# | patroni1 | Leader  | running   |
# | patroni2 | Replica | streaming |
# | patroni3 | Replica | streaming |

# NOTE: If your leader is different (e.g., patroni2 or patroni3),
# replace 'patroni1' in the stop command below accordingly.

items.label: Step 9b: Stop the leader process (simulate crash)
items.code:
# STOP THE LEADER — simulate a crash:
sudo systemctl stop patroni1

# Expected:
# (no output — run the next command to check status)

# Verify it stopped:
sudo systemctl status patroni1 --no-pager | grep Active
# Expected: Active: inactive (dead)

# What just happened:
# 1. Patroni on patroni1 stopped heartbeating to etcd
# 2. After ~10 seconds (loop_wait), the other Patroni nodes detect the lock is stale
# 3. etcd releases the leader lock
# 4. The remaining Patroni nodes hold a new election
# 5. One replica is promoted to leader
# 6. HAProxy detects the new leader via /primary health check

items.label: Step 9c: Verify the cluster auto-recovered
items.code:
# Wait 15 seconds for failover to complete:
echo "Waiting 15 seconds for failover..."
sleep 15

# Check the new cluster state:
sudo -u postgres patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni2.yml list

# Expected:
# + Cluster: pg-ha-cluster ----------+---------+-----------+----+-----------+
# | Member   | Host             | Role    | State     | TL | Lag in MB |
# +----------+------------------+---------+-----------+----+-----------+
# | patroni1 | 127.0.0.1:15432  |         | stopped   |  1 |           |
# | patroni2 | 127.0.0.1:15433  | Leader  | running   |  2 |           |
# | patroni3 | 127.0.0.1:15434  | Replica | streaming |  2 |         0 |
# +----------+------------------+---------+-----------+----+-----------+

# KEY OBSERVATIONS:
# • patroni2 was promoted from Replica to Leader
# • Timeline advanced from 1 to 2 (each failover creates a new timeline)
# • patroni3 is still streaming from the new leader (patroni2)
# • patroni1 shows as 'stopped' — it was the old leader

items.label: Step 9d: Verify data survived the failover
items.code:
# Connect to the NEW leader through HAProxy (still on port 5000):
psql -h localhost -p 5000 -U postgres -d ha_test

# Password: super-pass-789

-- Check our data is still there:
SELECT * FROM users;

# Expected:
#  id |  name   |       email        |         created_at
# ----+---------+--------------------+----------------------------
#   1 | Alice   | alice@example.com  | 2026-05-21 10:00:00.123456
#   2 | Bob     | bob@example.com    | bob@example.com
#   3 | Charlie | charlie@example.com | charlie@example.com
# (3 rows)

-- Verify we are indeed on the new leader:
SELECT pg_is_in_recovery();

# Expected:
#  pg_is_in_recovery
# -------------------
#  f
# (1 row)

-- Add more data on the new leader:
INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES ('Dave', 'dave@example.com');

\q

items.label: Step 9e: Test HAProxy read port still works
items.code:
# Connect to the read port (5001) — load-balanced to replicas:
psql -h localhost -p 5001 -U postgres -d ha_test

# Password: super-pass-789

SELECT * FROM users;

# Expected: all 4 rows including Dave

SELECT pg_is_in_recovery();

# Expected: 't' (true = this is a replica, read-only)

\q

subsections.title: Step 10: Test Node Recovery (Rejoin Old Leader)
content: When a failed node comes back, Patroni should automatically rejoin it as a replica. Patroni uses pg_rewind to synchronize the old leader's data with the new leader's timeline.

items.label: Step 10a: Bring back the old leader service
items.code:
# Start the previously stopped Patroni service:
sudo systemctl start patroni1

# Expected:
# (no output — check with status)

# Verify it started:
sudo systemctl status patroni1 --no-pager | grep Active
# Expected: Active: active (running)

items.label: Step 10b: Watch it rejoin the cluster
items.code:
# Wait 20 seconds for Patroni to initialize and rejoin:
echo "Waiting 20 seconds for recovery..."
sleep 20

# Check cluster state:
sudo -u postgres patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni2.yml list

# Expected:
# + Cluster: pg-ha-cluster ----------+---------+-----------+----+-----------+
# | Member   | Host             | Role    | State     | TL | Lag in MB |
# +----------+------------------+---------+-----------+----+-----------+
# | patroni1 | 127.0.0.1:15432  | Replica | streaming |  2 |         0 |
# | patroni2 | 127.0.0.1:15433  | Leader  | running   |  2 |           |
# | patroni3 | 127.0.0.1:15434  | Replica | streaming |  2 |         0 |
# +----------+------------------+---------+-----------+----+-----------+

# KEY OBSERVATIONS:
# • patroni1 rejoined as a 'Replica' (not as Leader)
# • patroni2 remains the Leader
# • patroni1 caught up on timeline 2 (it was on timeline 1 when it crashed)
# • pg_rewind automatically synchronized the old leader's data

items.label: Step 10c: Verify the recovered node has all data
items.code:
# Connect directly to the recovered node (patroni1 on port 15432):
psql -h localhost -p 15432 -U postgres -d ha_test

# Password: super-pass-789

SELECT * FROM users;

# Expected: all 4 rows including 'Dave' (inserted while patroni1 was down)
# This proves WAL streaming caught it up to the new leader

SELECT pg_is_in_recovery();

# Expected: 't' (it is now a replica)

\q

subsections.title: Step 11: Test Graceful Switchover (Planned Maintenance)
content: Switchover is a controlled leader change, unlike failover which is unplanned. Use this for patching, upgrades, or hardware maintenance.

items.label: Step 11a: Check current leader
items.code:
# Before switchover:
sudo -u postgres patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni2.yml list

# Current leader (let us assume it is patroni2 after the failover test)
# We will switch from patroni2 to patroni3

items.label: Step 11b: Perform the switchover
items.code:
# Graceful switchover: move leader role from patroni2 to patroni3:
sudo -u postgres patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni2.yml switchover --leader patroni2 --candidate patroni3 --force

# Expected output:
# Success: data is now consistent across the cluster
# Lock owner: patroni3; I am now following a different leader.
# patroni3: promoted
# + Cluster: pg-ha-cluster ----------+---------+-----------+----+-----------+
# | Member   | Host             | Role    | State     | TL | Lag      |
# +----------+------------------+---------+-----------+----+-----------+
# | patroni1 | 127.0.0.1:15432  | Replica | streaming |  3 |        0 |
# | patroni2 | 127.0.0.1:15433  | Replica | streaming |  3 |        0 |
# | patroni3 | 127.0.0.1:15434  | Leader  | running   |  3 |          |
# +----------+------------------+---------+-----------+----+-----------+

items.label: Step 11c: Verify no data loss after switchover
items.code:
# Connect through HAProxy write port:
psql -h localhost -p 5000 -U postgres -d ha_test

# Password: super-pass-789

SELECT * FROM users;
# Should still show all 4 rows

-- Verify we are on the new leader (patroni3):
SELECT inet_server_addr(), inet_server_port();
# Expected: (127.0.0.1, 15434)

\q

subsections.title: Step 12: Test Read/Write Splitting
content: One of the key benefits of HAProxy is read/write splitting. Writes always go to the leader. Reads can be distributed across all replicas for load balancing.

items.label: Test write routing (port 5000)
items.code:
# Write port should always connect to the leader:
for i in 1 2 3; do
    psql -h localhost -p 5000 -U postgres -d ha_test -t -c "SELECT inet_server_addr(), inet_server_port(), 'write' as port_type;"
done

# Expected: all 3 queries return the same server (the current leader)
# Example:
#  127.0.0.1 | 15434 | write  (patroni3 is the leader)
#  127.0.0.1 | 15434 | write
#  127.0.0.1 | 15434 | write

items.label: Test read routing (port 5001)
items.code:
# Read port should round-robin across replicas:
for i in 1 2 3; do
    psql -h localhost -p 5001 -U postgres -d ha_test -t -c "SELECT inet_server_addr(), inet_server_port(), 'read' as port_type;"
done

# Expected: queries return different servers in round-robin order
# Example:
#  127.0.0.1 | 15432 | read  (patroni1)
#  127.0.0.1 | 15433 | read  (patroni2)
#  127.0.0.1 | 15434 | read  (patroni3 — leader also serves reads)

# NOTE: The leader also returns HTTP 200 on /replica endpoint,
# so it participates in read load-balancing too.

items.label: Verify writes fail on read port (important safety check)
items.code:
# Try writing to the read-only port:
psql -h localhost -p 5001 -U postgres -d ha_test -c "INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES ('Eve', 'eve@example.com');"

# Expected:
# ERROR:  cannot execute INSERT in a read-only transaction

# This confirms the read port correctly routes to replicas,
# which reject writes. Write safety is enforced by PostgreSQL itself.

subsections.title: Step 13: View HAProxy Stats Dashboard
subsections.content: HAProxy includes a real-time statistics dashboard that shows the status of every backend server.

items.label: Open the stats dashboard
items.code:
# Option 1: Command line
curl -s -u admin:admin http://localhost:8404/ | grep -E '(UP|DOWN|MAINT|status)' | head -20

# Option 2: Open in browser
# Navigate to: http://localhost:8404/
# Username: admin
# Password: admin

# The dashboard shows:
# • pg-write backend: one server UP (the leader), two servers DOWN
# • pg-read backend: all three servers UP
# • Session rates, bytes in/out, connection counts

subsections.title: Clean Up
subsections.content: When you are done testing, stop and clean up the lab environment.

items.label: Stop all services and remove data
items.code:
# Stop all services:
sudo systemctl stop patroni1 patroni2 patroni3 haproxy etcd1 etcd2 etcd3

# Disable services (optional):
sudo systemctl disable patroni1 patroni2 patroni3 haproxy etcd1 etcd2 etcd3

# Remove data directories (WARNING: destroys all data):
sudo rm -rf /var/lib/postgresql/patroni*
sudo rm -rf /var/lib/etcd/etcd*

# Remove configuration files:
sudo rm -rf /etc/patroni
sudo rm -f /etc/etcd{1,2,3}.conf.yml

# Remove service files:
sudo rm -f /etc/systemd/system/patroni{1,2,3}.service
sudo rm -f /etc/systemd/system/etcd{1,2,3}.service
sudo systemctl daemon-reload

sections.title: Method 2: pg_auto_failover (Simpler Alternative)
content: pg_auto_failover is a PostgreSQL extension and service developed by Microsoft's Citus team. It provides automated failover with a simpler architecture than Patroni: just a monitor node and PostgreSQL data nodes. There is no separate etcd cluster — the monitor itself handles coordination.
This approach is great for testing because the setup requires fewer components (no etcd, no HAProxy), yet it still provides real automated failover with synchronous replication.

subsections.title: Architecture: How pg_auto_failover Works
content: pg_auto_failover uses a three-node architecture: one monitor and two (or more) PostgreSQL data nodes. The monitor runs a lightweight PostgreSQL instance that tracks the state of all data nodes. Each data node runs a 'keeper' process that reports its health to the monitor and executes the monitor's commands (promote, demote, etc.).

By default, pg_auto_failover uses synchronous replication, which means every write waits for at least one replica to acknowledge it. This provides stronger data protection than Patroni's default async replication.

rows: Streaming replica, takes over on failover
Connects to primary for writes, standby for reads

subsections.title: Step 1: Install pg_auto_failover
content: pg_auto_failover is available as a PostgreSQL extension package. Install it alongside PostgreSQL 17.

items.label: Install the pg_auto_failover extension
items.code:
# Install pg_auto_failover package:
sudo apt install -y postgresql-17-pg-auto-failover

# Verify installation:
pg_autoctl --version
# Expected: pg_autoctl 2.x

# Check that the extension is available:
sudo -u postgres psql -c "SELECT * FROM pg_available_extensions WHERE name = 'pgautofailover';"
# Expected: one row showing pgautofailover extension

subsections.title: Step 2: Initialize the Monitor Node
subsections.content: The monitor uses its own PostgreSQL instance to track cluster state.

items.label: Create and start the monitor
items.code:
# Create the monitor data directory:
sudo mkdir -p /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-monitor
sudo chown postgres:postgres /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-monitor

# Initialize the monitor:
sudo -u postgres pg_autoctl create monitor \
    --pgdata /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-monitor \
    --pgport 15432 \
    --auth trust \
    --ssl-self-signed \
    --run

# Expected output:
# 12:34:56 INFO  Creating monitor in /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-monitor
# 12:34:58 INFO  Monitor is ready
# 12:34:58 INFO  pg_autoctl is running in the foreground

# Press Ctrl+C to stop (we will set it up as a background service in the next step)

items.label: Start the monitor as a background service
items.code:
# Run the monitor in the background as a service:
sudo -u postgres pg_autoctl run \
    --pgdata /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-monitor \
    --pgport 15432 &

# Wait for it to start:
sleep 5

# Verify it is running:
psql -h localhost -p 15432 -U monitor -d monitor -c "SELECT 1;"
# Expected: (1 row)

subsections.title: Step 3: Initialize and Start the Data Nodes
content: Node 1 starts as the primary. Node 2 starts as a standby (secondary) and will be promoted if the primary fails.

items.label: Create Node 1 (primary)
items.code:
# Create the node 1 data directory:
sudo mkdir -p /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node1
sudo chown postgres:postgres /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node1

# Initialize node 1 as a PostgreSQL data node:
sudo -u postgres pg_autoctl create postgres \
    --pgdata /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node1 \
    --pgport 15433 \
    --name node1 \
    --auth trust \
    --ssl-self-signed \
    --monitor "postgresql://autoctl_node@localhost:15432/pg_auto_failover?sslmode=require" \
    --run

# Expected:
# 12:35:00 INFO  Creating postgres in /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node1
# 12:35:10 INFO  PostgreSQL is ready
# 12:35:10 INFO  This node is the primary
# 12:35:10 INFO  pg_autoctl is running in the foreground

# Press Ctrl+C and run it in background instead:

items.label: Run Node 1 in the background
items.code:
sudo -u postgres pg_autoctl run \
    --pgdata /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node1 \
    --pgport 15433 &

sleep 5

# Verify it is primary:
psql -h localhost -p 15433 -U pgaf_test -d pgaf_test -c "SELECT pg_is_in_recovery();"
# Expected: f (false = primary)

items.label: Create Node 2 (standby)
items.code:
# Create the node 2 data directory:
sudo mkdir -p /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node2
sudo chown postgres:postgres /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node2

# Initialize node 2 as a standby:
sudo -u postgres pg_autoctl create postgres \
    --pgdata /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node2 \
    --pgport 15434 \
    --name node2 \
    --auth trust \
    --ssl-self-signed \
    --monitor "postgresql://autoctl_node@localhost:15432/pg_auto_failover?sslmode=require" \
    --run

# Expected:
# 12:36:00 INFO  Creating postgres in /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node2
# 12:36:10 INFO  Synchronous streaming is active
# 12:36:10 INFO  This node is a secondary
# 12:36:10 INFO  pg_autoctl is running in the foreground

# Press Ctrl+C and run it in background:

items.label: Run Node 2 in the background
items.code:
sudo -u postgres pg_autoctl run \
    --pgdata /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node2 \
    --pgport 15434 &

sleep 5

# Verify it is a standby:
psql -h localhost -p 15434 -U pgaf_test -d pgaf_test -c "SELECT pg_is_in_recovery();"
# Expected: t (true = standby)

items.label: View cluster state
items.code:
# Check the full cluster state:
sudo -u postgres pg_autoctl show state --pgdata /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node1

# Expected:
#  Name |  Node |      Host:Port       |  TLI |  Health   |       Role
# ------+-------+----------------------+------+-----------+------------------
# node1 |     1 | localhost:15433      |    1 |      good |           primary
# node2 |     2 | localhost:15434      |    1 |      good |          secondary
# ------+-------+----------------------+------+-----------+------------------
# monitor|       | localhost:15432      |      |           |              n/a

items.label: Insert data on the primary
Trigger a switchover (controlled failover test)
items.code:
# pg_auto_failover supports graceful switchover without stopping anything:
sudo -u postgres pg_autoctl perform switchover --pgdata /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node1

# Expected output:
# 12:34:56 INFO  Switchover to node2 completed

# Check the new state:
sudo -u postgres pg_autoctl show state --pgdata /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node2

# Expected:
#  Name |  Node |      Host:Port       |  TLI |  Health   |       Role
# ------+-------+----------------------+------+-----------+------------------
# node1 |     1 | localhost:15433      |    2 |      good |          secondary
# node2 |     2 | localhost:15434      |    2 |      good |           primary

items.label: Verify data survives switchover
items.code:
# Connect to the new primary (node2, port 15434):
psql -h localhost -p 15434 -U pgaf_test -d pgaf_test -c "SELECT * FROM failover_test;"

# Expected: all 3 rows are still there

# Insert more data:
psql -h localhost -p 15434 -U pgaf_test -d pgaf_test -c "INSERT INTO failover_test (message) VALUES ('After switchover!');"

# Verify the old primary (now standby) gets the new data:
psql -h localhost -p 15433 -U pgaf_test -d pgaf_test -c "SELECT * FROM failover_test;"
# Expected: 4 rows, including 'After switchover!'

subsections.title: Step 7: Test Crash Recovery (Stop the Primary)

items.label: Stop the primary process
items.code:
# Find the PID of the primary (node2) and stop it to simulate a crash:
NODE2_PID=$(sudo -u postgres pg_autoctl show state --pgdata /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node2 --json | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['nodes'][0]['pid'])")
sudo kill -9 $NODE2_PID

# Wait for failover:
echo "Waiting 15 seconds for automatic failover..."
sleep 15

# Check state via the monitor:
sudo -u postgres pg_autoctl show state --pgdata /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node1
# Expected: node1 has been promoted to primary

items.label: Restart the failed node and bring it back
items.code:
# Restart node2 as a standby:
sudo -u postgres pg_autoctl run \
    --pgdata /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node2 \
    --pgport 15434 &

sleep 15

# Check state:
sudo -u postgres pg_autoctl show state --pgdata /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-node1

# Expected: node2 rejoins as secondary
#  node1 | primary
#  node2 | secondary
# The monitor automatically reintegrates the recovered node

items.label: Stop and remove all data
items.code:
# Stop all pg_autoctl processes:
sudo pkill -f pg_autoctl

# Remove data directories:
sudo rm -rf /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-*

echo "pg_auto_failover lab cleaned up"

sections.title: Method 3: Manual Streaming Replication (Lightweight)
content: For the simplest possible HA test, you can set up streaming replication between two native PostgreSQL instances manually. This approach has no automatic failover — you promote a replica manually — but it is excellent for learning how PostgreSQL replication actually works under the hood.
This is the fastest to set up, uses only the PostgreSQL you already installed, and requires the least configuration.

subsections.title: Step 1: Create Data Directories and Configuration
content: We will create two PostgreSQL instances: primary (port 15435) and standby (port 15436). Each gets its own data directory, configuration, and systemd service.

items.label: Create directories for both instances
items.code:
sudo mkdir -p /var/lib/postgresql/primary
sudo mkdir -p /var/lib/postgresql/standby
sudo chown postgres:postgres /var/lib/postgresql/primary /var/lib/postgresql/standby

items.label: Initialize the primary data directory
items.code:
# Initialize the primary instance:
sudo -u postgres /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin/initdb \
    -D /var/lib/postgresql/primary \
    --encoding=UTF8 \
    --data-checksums

# Expected:
# Success. You can now start the database server...

items.label: Configure primary for replication
items.code:
# Edit postgresql.conf on the primary:
sudo -u postgres tee -a /var/lib/postgresql/primary/postgresql.conf << 'EOF'
listen_addresses = '*'
port = 15435
wal_level = replica
max_wal_senders = 5
wal_keep_size = 256
hot_standby = on
max_connections = 50
EOF

# Add replication user to pg_hba.conf:
sudo -u postgres tee -a /var/lib/postgresql/primary/pg_hba.conf << 'EOF'
host replication replicator 127.0.0.1/32 md5
host all replicator 127.0.0.1/32 md5
host all all 127.0.0.1/32 md5
EOF

items.label: Start the primary PostgreSQL instance
items.code:
# Start the primary:
sudo -u postgres /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin/pg_ctl start \
    -D /var/lib/postgresql/primary \
    -l /var/lib/postgresql/primary/logfile

# Expected:
# waiting for server to start.... done
# server started

# Verify it is running:
psql -h localhost -p 15435 -U postgres -c "SELECT 1;"
# Expected: (1 row)

items.label: Create replication user and test data
items.code:
# Create a dedicated replication role:
psql -h localhost -p 15435 -U postgres -c "CREATE ROLE replicator WITH REPLICATION LOGIN PASSWORD 'replpass';"

# Create a test database and table:
psql -h localhost -p 15435 -U postgres -c "CREATE DATABASE testdb;"
psql -h localhost -p 15435 -U postgres -d testdb << 'SQL'
CREATE TABLE items (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT NOT NULL,
    value INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);
INSERT INTO items (name, value) VALUES
    ('Alpha', 100),
    ('Beta',  200),
    ('Gamma', 300);
SQL

# Verify:
psql -h localhost -p 15435 -U postgres -d testdb -c "SELECT * FROM items;"
# Expected: Alpha, Beta, Gamma

subsections.title: Step 2: Create a Replication Slot
subsections.content: A replication slot ensures the primary retains WAL segments until the standby has received them.

items.label: Create a physical replication slot
items.code:
# Create a replication slot for the standby:
psql -h localhost -p 15435 -U postgres -c "SELECT pg_create_physical_replication_slot('standby_slot');"

# Expected:
#  pg_create_physical_replication_slot
# -------------------------------------
#  (standby_slot,)
# (1 row)

# Verify:
psql -h localhost -p 15435 -U postgres -c "SELECT slot_name, slot_type, active FROM pg_replication_slots;"
# Expected:
#   slot_name    | slot_type | active
# ----------------+-----------+--------
#  standby_slot  | physical  | f

subsections.title: Step 3: Set Up the Standby with pg_basebackup
content: We clone the primary's data directory using pg_basebackup, which creates a ready-to-use standby.

items.label: Clone primary data to standby directory
items.code:
# Clone the primary using pg_basebackup:
sudo -u postgres /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin/pg_basebackup \
    -h localhost -p 15435 -U replicator \
    -D /var/lib/postgresql/standby \
    -Fp -Xs -R -P -v \
    --slot=standby_slot

# When prompted for password, type: replpass

# Expected output:
# Password:
# pg_basebackup: initiating base backup, waiting for checkpoint to complete
# pg_basebackup: checkpoint completed
# pg_basebackup: write-ahead log start point: 0/2000028 on timeline 1
# pg_basebackup: starting background WAL receiver
# pg_basebackup: base backup completed

# The -R flag creates standby.signal and writes primary_conninfo automatically.

items.label: Configure standby port
items.code:
# The -R flag creates standby.signal but we need to set the port:
sudo -u postgres tee -a /var/lib/postgresql/standby/postgresql.conf << 'EOF'
port = 15436
listen_addresses = '*'
max_connections = 50
EOF

# Check the primary_conninfo was written:
sudo cat /var/lib/postgresql/standby/postgresql.auto.conf
# Expected: primary_conninfo = '...' with host=localhost port=15435

items.label: Start the standby PostgreSQL instance
items.code:
# Start the standby:
sudo -u postgres /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin/pg_ctl start \
    -D /var/lib/postgresql/standby \
    -l /var/lib/postgresql/standby/logfile

# Expected:
# waiting for server to start.... done
# server started

# Wait for it to connect and start streaming:
sleep 5

items.label: Verify replication is working
items.code:
# Check replication status on the primary:
psql -h localhost -p 15435 -U postgres -c "\x" -c "SELECT * FROM pg_stat_replication;"

# Expected (expanded display):
# -[ RECORD 1 ]----+------------------------------
# pid              | 12345
# usename          | replicator
# application_name | walreceiver
# state            | streaming
# sync_state       | async

# Verify the standby has our test data:
psql -h localhost -p 15436 -U postgres -d testdb -c "SELECT * FROM items;"
# Expected: Alpha, Beta, Gamma

# Check standby is in recovery:
psql -h localhost -p 15436 -U postgres -c "SELECT pg_is_in_recovery();"
# Expected: t (true)

subsections.title: Step 4: Test Manual Failover (Promote Standby)
content: Without Patroni or pg_auto_failover, failover is manual. You promote the standby to primary using pg_ctl promote.

items.label: Step 4a: Insert more data on primary
items.code:
psql -h localhost -p 15435 -U postgres -d testdb -c "INSERT INTO items (name, value) VALUES ('Delta', 400);"

# Wait for WAL to be sent:
sleep 2

items.label: Step 4b: Promote the standby to primary
items.code:
# Simulate primary failure by stopping it:
sudo -u postgres /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin/pg_ctl stop \
    -D /var/lib/postgresql/primary

# Expected:
# waiting for server to shut down.... done
# server stopped

# Promote the standby:
sudo -u postgres /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin/pg_ctl promote \
    -D /var/lib/postgresql/standby

# Expected:
# waiting for server to promote.... done
# server promoted

# Verify the standby is now writable:
psql -h localhost -p 15436 -U postgres -c "SELECT pg_is_in_recovery();"
# Expected: f (false = now primary)

# Check our data:
psql -h localhost -p 15436 -U postgres -d testdb -c "SELECT * FROM items;"
# Expected: Alpha, Beta, Gamma, Delta (4 rows)

items.label: Step 4c: Insert data on the new primary
items.code:
psql -h localhost -p 15436 -U postgres -d testdb -c "INSERT INTO items (name, value) VALUES ('Epsilon', 500);"

psql -h localhost -p 15436 -U postgres -d testdb -c "SELECT * FROM items;"
# Expected: 5 rows including Epsilon

subsections.title: Step 5: Reconfigure Old Primary as Standby

items.label: Make the old primary a standby of the new primary
items.code:
# Remove old primary's data:
sudo -u postgres rm -rf /var/lib/postgresql/primary/*

# Re-clone from the new primary (now on port 15436):
sudo -u postgres /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin/pg_basebackup \
    -h localhost -p 15436 -U replicator \
    -D /var/lib/postgresql/primary \
    -Fp -Xs -R -P -v

# When prompted for password, type: replpass

# Set the port for the old primary (now a standby):
sudo -u postgres tee -a /var/lib/postgresql/primary/postgresql.conf << 'EOF'
port = 15435
listen_addresses = '*'
max_connections = 50
EOF

# Start it as a standby:
sudo -u postgres /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin/pg_ctl start \
    -D /var/lib/postgresql/primary \
    -l /var/lib/postgresql/primary/logfile

sleep 5

# Verify it catches up:
psql -h localhost -p 15435 -U postgres -d testdb -c "SELECT * FROM items;"
# Expected: 5 rows (catches up with the new primary)

psql -h localhost -p 15435 -U postgres -c "SELECT pg_is_in_recovery();"
# Expected: t (now a standby)

items.label: Stop and remove all data
items.code:
# Stop both instances:
sudo -u postgres /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin/pg_ctl stop -D /var/lib/postgresql/primary
sudo -u postgres /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin/pg_ctl stop -D /var/lib/postgresql/standby

# Remove data directories:
sudo rm -rf /var/lib/postgresql/primary /var/lib/postgresql/standby

echo "Manual replication lab cleaned up"

sections.title: Approach Comparison: Which Method Should You Use?
content: Each approach serves a different purpose. Here is a comparison to help you choose.

sections.title: What You CAN and CANNOT Test on a Single VM
content: A single VM with multiple native PostgreSQL instances is excellent for testing the logical behavior of HA — failover timing, leader election, read/write routing, and node recovery. However, it cannot simulate certain physical infrastructure failures. Understanding these limitations is critical before you move to production.

rows: systemctl stop patroni1 (or kill -9 the PG process)
Patroni detects missing heartbeat, promotes replica
Stop leader, observe which replica wins
Distributed consensus via etcd works correctly
Patroni reports lag, blocks promotion if beyond threshold
Stop leader, failover, restart old leader
Old leader resyncs using pg_rewind, no data corruption
Leader transitions with zero data loss, minimal downtime
Writes go to leader, reads load-balanced across replicas
HAProxy handles connection pooling, PG handles max_connections
Replica catches up using WAL streaming

items.label: ❌ CANNOT Test on a Single VM

rows: All instances share the same loopback interface on 127.0.0.1. You can stop one process, but can't split the network.
Use separate VMs with iptables to simulate partition, or use tools like Toxiproxy (see note below).
All instances share the same host filesystem (even with separate directories, they are on the same physical disk).
Use separate VMs with different storage devices, or use a cloud provider's fault injection service.
All processes share the host kernel's memory. OOM behavior differs from separate machines.
Use systemd memory limits (MemoryMax=) combined with stress tests.
All processes compete for the same CPU cores. Replication lag may be artificially high or low.
Use separate VMs with dedicated CPUs, or systemd CPUAccounting for better isolation.
All processes stop at once when the host shuts down.
Test with actual hardware or cloud instances with dedicated power management.
All processes use the same I/O stack. One instance's slow I/O affects others.
Use separate disks or cloud volumes with different performance profiles.
Difficult to create because all processes share the same network and storage.
Requires complex network manipulation (e.g., iptables rules on the host, traffic shaping).
Latency between processes on localhost is microseconds, not milliseconds.
Use traffic shaping tools (tc) to add latency, but results are still artificial.

items.label: 💡 Tip: Better Isolation for Advanced Testing
text: If you need to test network partitions or latency more realistically on a single machine, consider:
1. Toxiproxy (https://github.com/Shopify/toxiproxy) — proxy that injects failures: latency, disconnects, bandwidth limits. Great for testing how Patroni handles network degradation.
2. systemd resource controls: use `MemoryMax`, `CPUQuota`, and `IOWeight` in service files to limit individual services.
3. iptables on the host: use `iptables -A INPUT -s <IP> -j DROP` to simulate network failures between processes on different ports.
These tools help bridge the gap between single-VM testing and production behavior.

sections.title: Best Practices for Single-VM HA Testing
content: Follow these practices to get the most out of your single-VM HA testing.

items.label: 1. Set Resource Limits on Services
text: Without resource limits, one PostgreSQL instance can consume all host memory and cause other processes to be OOM-killed. This creates false failures that do not reflect real HA behavior.
Use systemd resource controls to limit each service:
items.code:
# Add these to each patroni service file under [Service]:
[Service]
MemoryMax=2G
CPUQuota=200%
IOWeight=100

# Also set PostgreSQL memory parameters in Patroni YAML:
postgresql:
  parameters:
    shared_buffers: 256MB
    effective_cache_size: 768MB
    work_mem: 32MB
    maintenance_work_mem: 64MB
    max_connections: 50

# For etcd services:
[Service]
MemoryMax=256M
CPUQuota=50%

items.label: 2. Use Separate Data Directories
text: Each PostgreSQL instance must use its own data directory. Never share a data directory between instances.
items.code:
# Correct — separate directories:
/var/lib/postgresql/patroni1/
/var/lib/postgresql/patroni2/
/var/lib/postgresql/patroni3/

# Incorrect — all nodes sharing one dir (data corruption risk):
/var/lib/postgresql/shared/  # NEVER do this

items.label: 3. Avoid Port Conflicts
text: On a single VM, every process needs unique ports. Use different ports for each PostgreSQL and Patroni instance. Keep a mapping table.

items.label: 4. Use systemd for Service Management
text: Systemd service files make it easy to start, stop, and monitor each component. Enable journald logging for centralized log access.
items.code:
# Check status of all services:
sudo systemctl status patroni1 patroni2 patroni3 etcd1 etcd2 etcd3 haproxy --no-pager

# View logs for a specific service:
sudo journalctl -u patroni1 -f

# Filter failover events:
sudo journalctl -u patroni1 | grep -iE "(leader|lock|election|promote|failover)"
sudo journalctl -u etcd1 | grep -iE "(leader|election|raft)"

items.label: 5. Test with Automation Scripts
text: Write scripts that automate the failover tests so you can repeat them consistently.
items.code:
#!/bin/bash
# test-failover.sh — automated failover test

set -euo pipefail

LEADER_BEFORE=$(sudo -u postgres patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni1.yml list | grep Leader | awk '{print $2}')
echo "Current leader: $LEADER_BEFORE"

echo "=== Test 1: Stopping leader ==="
sudo systemctl stop "$LEADER_BEFORE"
sleep 15

LEADER_AFTER=$(sudo -u postgres patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni2.yml list | grep Leader | awk '{print $2}')
echo "New leader: $LEADER_AFTER"

if [ "$LEADER_BEFORE" = "$LEADER_AFTER" ]; then
    echo "FAIL: Leader did not change!"
    exit 1
fi
echo "PASS: Failover completed successfully"

echo "=== Test 2: Restart old leader ==="
sudo systemctl start "$LEADER_BEFORE"
sleep 20

REJOINED=$(sudo -u postgres patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni2.yml list | grep "$LEADER_BEFORE" | awk '{print $3}')
echo "$LEADER_BEFORE role: $REJOINED"

if [ "$REJOINED" != "Replica" ]; then
    echo "FAIL: Old leader did not rejoin as replica!"
    exit 1
fi
echo "PASS: Old leader rejoined as replica"

echo "=== All tests passed! ==="

items.label: 6. Monitor Process Logs
text: When testing failover, watch process logs in real time to understand what Patroni is doing.
items.code:
# Watch Patroni logs during failover:
sudo journalctl -u patroni1 -u patroni2 -u patroni3 -f

# Filter for failover-related events:
sudo journalctl -u patroni1 | grep -iE "(leader|lock|election|promote|failover)"

# Watch etcd for lock changes:
sudo journalctl -u etcd1 | grep -iE "(leader|election|raft)"

items.label: 7. Clean Up Regularly
text: PostgreSQL data directories accumulate WAL files over time. Regular cleanup prevents disk exhaustion.
items.code:
# Stop all services:
sudo systemctl stop patroni1 patroni2 patroni3 haproxy etcd1 etcd2 etcd3

# Remove all data:
sudo rm -rf /var/lib/postgresql/patroni*
sudo rm -rf /var/lib/etcd/etcd*

# Check disk usage:
df -h /var/lib/postgresql /
du -sh /var/lib/postgresql/*

sections.title: Troubleshooting Common Issues
content: Here are the most common issues when testing PostgreSQL HA on a single VM with native services.

items.label: Issue 1: Patroni fails to start with 'Failed to bootstrap cluster'
text: Cause: Wrong bin_dir or missing PostgreSQL binaries.
Solution: Check the PostgreSQL version installed. The bin_dir must match:
PostgreSQL 16 → /usr/lib/postgresql/16/bin
PostgreSQL 17 → /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin
PostgreSQL 18 → /usr/lib/postgresql/18/bin
Fix by setting the correct bin_dir in your patroni YAML configuration.

items.label: Issue 2: ETCD cluster fails to form (only see 1 member)
text: Cause: ETCD nodes cannot reach each other. On a single VM this is usually because:
• The initial-cluster has incorrect port numbers
• A previous cluster left stale data in the data directory
• Port conflicts with another service
Solution:
1. Verify each etcd config uses a unique data-dir and ports
2. Remove old data: sudo rm -rf /var/lib/etcd/etcd*
3. Verify: etcdctl --endpoints=http://127.0.0.1:12379 member list
4. Check logs: sudo journalctl -u etcd1 --no-pager | tail -30

items.label: Issue 3: HAProxy shows all servers DOWN
text: Cause: HAProxy cannot reach Patroni REST API.
Solution:
1. Verify Patroni REST API is accessible: curl -s http://127.0.0.1:18081/health
2. Check HAProxy config has correct IP:port for each server
3. Ensure Patroni is running: sudo systemctl status patroni1
4. Check HAProxy logs: sudo journalctl -u haproxy --no-pager | tail -20

items.label: Issue 4: Replication lag is high or growing
text: Cause: Insufficient resources. On a single VM with 3 PostgreSQL instances, the CPUs and I/O are shared.
Solutions:
• Reduce shared_buffers and work_mem in each PostgreSQL instance
• Limit max_connections (e.g., 50 per node instead of 200)
• Set wal_keep_size to a smaller value (e.g., 128MB)
• Use systemd CPUQuota and MemoryMax to prevent resource starvation
• Monitor with: sudo -u postgres patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni1.yml list

items.label: Issue 5: Disk runs out of space
text: Cause: PostgreSQL WAL files accumulate in each data directory.
Solution:
• Set wal_keep_size to a reasonable value (256MB is enough for testing)
• Regularly clean up old data directories
• Monitor disk usage: df -h && du -sh /var/lib/postgresql/*
• Archive old test data before running new experiments

items.label: Issue 6: Cannot connect to psql
text: Cause: PostgreSQL authentication rejects the connection, or the service is not running.
Solution:
1. Verify the service is running: sudo systemctl status patroni1
2. Check pg_hba.conf allows connections from 127.0.0.1
3. Verify password: psql -h localhost -p 15432 -U postgres -W
4. Check Patroni logs: sudo journalctl -u patroni1 --no-pager | tail -30
5. Try connecting via local socket: sudo -u postgres psql -p 15432

items.label: Issue 7: pg_auto_failover monitor fails to start
text: Cause: The monitor's PostgreSQL instance fails to initialize, usually due to port conflicts or data directory permissions.
Solution:
1. Check logs: sudo journalctl -u pgaf-monitor --no-pager | tail -30
2. Ensure no other PostgreSQL is running on port 15432
3. Remove old data: sudo rm -rf /var/lib/postgresql/pgaf-*
4. Try again with clean state

items.label: Issue 8: Patroni keeps restarting (crash loop)
text: Cause: Patroni finds a stale lock in etcd from a previous cluster state.
Solution:
• Remove etcd data and restart clean: sudo systemctl stop etcd{1,2,3}; sudo rm -rf /var/lib/etcd/etcd*; sudo systemctl start etcd{1,2,3}
• Or clear just the Patroni keys in etcd: etcdctl --endpoints=http://127.0.0.1:12379 del /db/pg-ha-cluster --prefix

sections.title: Summary
content: You now have three different ways to test PostgreSQL High Availability on a single VM using native OS services. Each approach builds on the same core concepts — streaming replication, leader election, failover, and recovery — but at different levels of complexity and production realism.

On a single VM you can fully validate the logical behavior of HA: automatic failover, switchover, node recovery, and read/write routing. You cannot simulate physical infrastructure failures like network partitions or disk failures, but with tools like Toxiproxy and systemd resource controls you can get closer. Running services natively is the most direct way to learn PostgreSQL HA — what you see is what you get, without any container abstraction layer.

items.label: Key Takeaways
text: ✅ You can run a full Patroni + etcd + HAProxy cluster on a single VM using 7 systemd services — no separate VMs needed
✅ Automatic failover works: kill the leader process, and a replica is promoted within ~15 seconds
✅ Node recovery works: restart the old leader, and it rejoins as a replica via pg_rewind
✅ Graceful switchover works: use patronictl switchover for planned maintenance with zero data loss
✅ Read/write splitting works: HAProxy routes writes to the leader (port 5000) and reads across replicas (port 5001)
✅ pg_auto_failover offers a simpler alternative with synchronous replication and built-in monitoring
✅ Manual streaming replication is the lightest option and teaches you the fundamentals of PostgreSQL replication
❌ You cannot test true network partitions, disk failures, or realistic timing on a single VM — use separate VMs or cloud instances for that

rows: sudo systemctl start etcd1 etcd2 etcd3 patroni1 patroni2 patroni3 haproxy
sudo systemctl stop <leader> && sleep 15 && patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni2.yml list
sudo systemctl stop patroni1 patroni2 patroni3 haproxy etcd1 etcd2 etcd3

items.label: Next Steps
text: After mastering single-VM HA testing, here is what to explore next:
1. Add PgBouncer for connection pooling — install alongside HAProxy for production-grade connection management
2. Set up pgBackRest backup and WAL archiving — test Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR) alongside HA
3. Deploy the same stack to separate VMs or cloud instances — you will see how real network latency affects replication
4. Add monitoring with Prometheus + Grafana + postgres_exporter — visualize replication lag, connections, and failover events
5. Try chaos engineering with Toxiproxy — simulate network latency, disconnects, and bandwidth limits
6. Test with real traffic — use pgbench (built into PostgreSQL) to generate load and observe HA behavior under pressure
items.code:
# Quick pgbench test to generate load:
# Create a test database:
psql -h localhost -p 5000 -U postgres -c "CREATE DATABASE pgbench_test;"

# Initialize with 100,000 rows:
pgbench -h localhost -p 5000 -U postgres -i -s 10 pgbench_test

# Run a 60-second benchmark:
pgbench -h localhost -p 5000 -U postgres -T 60 -c 10 pgbench_test

# While pgbench is running, fail over the leader and watch transactions continue
